This is not about escaping work. It is about building something that reliably funds the moments that actually matter.
If you want to build a business so you can have more of this, and less of the desk, this is what we focus on inside FieldWork.

items.headline: When Your Business Runs Itself
items.angle: Use the meme format to contrast a common, high-stress online business emergency with the calm reality of someone whose business is automated. The humor and value come from showing a different, more resilient way to operate.
hooks: 🙄The algorithm just changed again.
🙄My main ad account just got shut down.
🙄Our primary lead source just dried up overnight.
🙄A key software we rely on just went under.
🙄My top funnel just completely broke.
talking_points: You just saw the text on screen: 'The algorithm just changed again.'
For most online business owners, that's a code red moment. It means panic, late nights, and scrambling to figure out the new rules before your leads disappear.
But my first thought wasn't about the algorithm. It was about what I was going to cook for dinner.
That is not because I do not care. It is because my business is built on systems that do not depend on any single platform.
We have several automated engines for lead generation, client onboarding, and content delivery that run in the background, insulated from any one platform's changes.
The goal is not to hustle harder when things break. The goal is to build a business so resilient that you barely notice when they do.
This is what 'Build Online, Live Offline' actually looks like. Your business should serve your life, not the other way around.
If you are tired of living in constant reaction mode and want to build a more durable business, I teach exactly how we do this inside my program, FieldWork.
You can learn more about it at the link in my bio.

items.headline: The New Game for Founders
items.angle: Reframe 'the game of business' from the typical hustle culture narrative into Christian's 'Build Online. Live Offline.' philosophy. Position the old way as a game you're forced to play, and FieldWork as the way to start playing a new, more fulfilling one.
hooks: They sold you one game. This is the other one.
This is the new playbook for founders. It's time to get outside.
Most online businesses are a trap. This is the escape hatch.
There are two ways to build. One chains you to a desk. This is the second way.
This is the work before the real work. Welcome.
talking_points: They sold us one version of the game. The one where you hustle, grind, and sacrifice everything for a business that ends up owning you.
That game promises freedom, but it usually just delivers a nicer office to feel trapped in. The scoreboard is always about more. More growth, more revenue, more time at the keyboard.
But there is another game you can play. It starts with a different question: what are you building this all for?
For us, the prize isn't endless growth. It's freedom. Real freedom. The kind that lets you shut the laptop on a Tuesday to take a hike, or be fully present with your family.
The way you win this new game is by building smart systems online that work for you, so you can spend more of your life offline, in the field.
You use smart automation and AI to build the machine once, so it can run without you.
This isn't about escaping work. It's about doing the right work so you can return to what matters most.
If you're an expert or a founder who feels stuck in the old game and you're ready to play a new one, this is your invitation. This is how we build it.

items.headline: The Get Offline Business
items.angle: Christian presents the core paradox of his philosophy: the entire purpose of building a smart online business is to spend less time online and more time living life offline.
hooks: The goal of my online business is to be less online.
My business grows the fastest when I step away from it.
I use robots and AI so I can be more human.
To scale my company, I had to learn how to leave it.
The most valuable asset in my business is my absence.
talking_points: The goal of my online business is to be less online.
Now, I know that sounds completely backward. We are constantly told that to succeed, we have to be always on, posting, replying, and grinding.
But that path just builds a digital prison. Your business, which was meant to create freedom, ends up chaining you to a screen.
The shift for me was realizing the point is not to build a business that you have to run every single day.
The point is to build a business that runs itself.
This means using smart automation and simple AI systems to handle the repeatable work, like client onboarding, follow ups, and even content distribution.
You build these operational engines once, and you build them well, so they can do the work for you.
This allows you to get back into the field, whether that's with your family, on a mountain, or just turning off your laptop at 3pm.
This is what we teach inside FieldWork. It's for founders and experts who want to build a business that buys back their time, not one that consumes it.

items.headline: The Day I Quit Being A Founder
items.angle: Frame Christian's 'rock bottom' moment not as a personal vice, but as burnout from hustle culture. The pivotal decision is to reject the 'always on' founder life and build a business that prioritizes living, which became the foundation for FieldWork.
hooks: It was a Tuesday in October, and I shut my laptop for the last time at 11 PM.
I remember the exact moment I realized I was building a prison instead of a business.
The date was September 28th. I missed my son's soccer game for a client call that didn't matter.
I was sitting in my home office on a perfect Saturday, and decided I was done trading my life for a living.
There was a specific Tuesday I looked at my calendar and realized the business owned me, not the other way around.
talking_points: It was a Tuesday night in late October, and as I shut my laptop at 11 PM, I knew I was done.
I had a massive task in front of me: figure out how to keep my business growing without letting it consume my entire life.
For years, I had bought into the idea that success meant sacrifice. I shut down my hobbies and missed moments with family to feed the business.
And when I looked at the future, I saw that the business I was building would eventually cost me everything that mattered.
Faced with that, I had two choices. I could keep grinding, burn out, and become a stranger to the people I love.
Or I could tear it all down and rebuild it the right way. A business designed to give me my time back, not take it.
So I did. I rebuilt everything around smart systems and automation. I focused only on the work that moved the needle.
The business grew faster than ever, and I got my life back. I was present for my family. I was back out in the field.
I don't know why it took me so long to see it, but I can tell you two things. It wouldn't have happened if I kept hustling, and it wouldn't have happened without building real systems.
That philosophy is exactly why I created FieldWork. If you're ready to build a business that serves your life, this is your invitation.
